news_summary: Today's technology news highlights the promising intersection of AI and philosophy as a means to address and solve major challenges in artificial intelligence.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant la convergence prometteuse entre l'IA et la philosophie, qui permet d'aborder et de résoudre les principaux défis liés à l'intelligence artificielle.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan la prometedora convergencia entre la inteligencia artificial y la filosofía como medio para abordar y resolver los principales retos de la inteligencia artificial.

articles.title: Can the biggest problems in AI be solved by philosophy?
articles.summary: AI companies are hiring philosophy graduates to help them understand the nature of consciousness, whether it can be replicated and how their systems can be made better and more reliable
articles.content:
AI companies are hiring philosophy graduates to help them understand the nature of consciousness, whether it can be replicated and how their systems can be made better and more reliable

AI think, therefore AI am Album / Alamy

Some of the biggest challenges in artificial intelligence are being worked on not by computer scientists head down in code but by philosophers lured from academia into jobs at AI firms. The philosophers are tasked with making the next generation of models more capable and reliable, but they also shed light on the mystery of consciousness and whether intelligence can be replicated in software alone.

Jonathan Birch at the London School of Economics and Political Science says AI companies are the big employers of philosophy PhDs right now, with offers of interesting work, large salaries and stock options proving too tempting for many to resist.

“Topics that have been researched in philosophy departments for decades – how to make rational decisions, how to systematise moral principles, what counts as thinking or reasoning or introspection, what counts as evidence of consciousness – are suddenly of massive value to AI companies,” says Birch. “So, naturally, we are seeing a huge brain drain.”

One of the key tasks for this crop of philosophers is alignment, the AI industry term for efforts to stop harmful content – such as revealing instructions on how to make bombs.

Efforts to stop AI models giving dangerous outputs were initially focused around putting in simple black-and-white guardrails, such as forbidding a model from talking about bombs entirely, for instance. But these proved clumsy and easy to circumvent. Now, companies are pursuing more advanced methods which lean heavily on philosophical understanding of right and wrong.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily The latest on what’s new in science and why it matters each day. Sign up to newsletter

However, it’s rarely straightforward. Researchers have found that if you tell a model to break a rule in one specific situation, it will start breaking lots of other rules, says Shane Glackin at the University of Exeter in the UK. And understanding why is exactly the sort of problem that philosophy’s logical analysis can unpick.

“The best explanation for that seems to be that there’s a semantic link deep in the corpus of texts it’s trained on that holds the good-coded things and the bad-coded things together. And once you allow it to do some bad, it picks up on that and extrapolates and starts doing other things that are bad,” says Glackin. “As an ethicist, one way of seeing what we’re doing is trying to work out the shape and the extent of concepts like right and wrong, or good and bad, and trying to work out what things fall under it, in common usage or conceptually, and that seems to be exactly the sort of analysis that the LLM is doing.”

Others key tasks for philosophers at AI companies include cutting down on hallucinations – the industry term for fabrications produced by models, generally improving performance and tackling biases inherent in the models. They also apply theories of human consciousness to AI models in an attempt to answer the persistent question of whether they display sentience.

“What do minds do, what do brains do, what can be replicated? This is a big issue for AIs. It’s a really important question for those companies to answer and it’s something that philosophers have been thinking about for a long, long time,” says Glackin.

The thorniest questions

Mahrad Almotahari at the University of Edinburgh, UK – who knows of two academics who’ve jumped ship to AI companies, and has also taken on advisory work for a commercial outfit himself – says that philosophy and computer science share a long history. In fact, the paper in which Alan Turing unveiled his famous test, known as the Turing test, to determine if a machine could display traits of intelligent beings was published in the philosophy journal Mind.

The exact scale of hiring is hard to estimate, but Aaron Kagan, chair of the American Philosophical Association’s Committee for Non-Academic Careers, has delved into job adverts for some insight. “A naive keyword count suggests 26.6 per cent of roles mention AI ethics, safety, alignment, governance or policy, but after removing boilerplate, only about 5 per cent substantively involve that work,” says Kagan.

Almotahari sees value in philosophy expertise for technology firms, but is sceptical that the thorniest questions of machine consciousness will be answered by them. Instead, he says philosophers could succeed in helping engineers to pick apart what’s going on inside models.

“There’s all this math taking place: can we extract from it a higher level description of what’s going on in terms of, say, this part of the model is representing that feature of the world, and this part is representing this other feature of the world?” asks Almotahari. “I think philosophers are well positioned to do that, to go from the engineering description to a representational description.”

Others see a potential problem on the horizon, with industry hiring philosophers eventually leading to biased research that serves the interests of technology companies.

“Going forward, it’s clear that a lot of serious philosophical work will be funded by industry. Explicitly or implicitly, companies have expectations they want to be fulfilled, and they have the power to favour authors who deliver welcome arguments and ideas,” says Birch. “I wish we had made more progress on the big questions of philosophy – about consciousness, agency, morality, etc – prior to the advent of AI. If we had, we would have been better prepared. AI has now given these questions massive urgency, and yet, the answers still seem far away.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful and positive development where philosophy graduates are being employed by AI companies to tackle significant challenges in AI such as ethical alignment, reducing harmful outputs, understanding consciousness, and improving model reliability. This interdisciplinary approach has broad implications for making AI safer and more trustworthy, which benefits society at large. The article provides substantial context on the roles philosophers play and the impact of their work, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Philosophers help AI companies solve big questions about thinking and safety
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming smarter but also faces challenges like making safe decisions and avoiding harmful content. To tackle these, AI companies are hiring philosophers, experts who study thinking, consciousness, and ethics.

**What happened** – Recently, many philosophy graduates have joined AI firms. Their job is to help explain what it means for machines to think and decide right from wrong. They work on making AI models more reliable and stopping them from saying dangerous or false things. Philosophers use their knowledge to guide AI developers on complex questions about intelligence and morality.

**Impact** – This is important because it brings new ideas to AI development. Philosophers help AI companies create better rules that are not easy to trick, improving the safety of AI systems. They also explore whether AI can be truly conscious or aware, a question that has puzzled scientists for years. This teamwork between philosophy and technology is shaping the future of AI in a thoughtful way.

**What's next step** – AI companies will continue to rely on philosophers to improve how AI understands ethical choices and to reduce mistakes like making up false information. Philosophers might also help explain how parts of AI models represent the world, making AI clearer and easier to trust. However, there is a need to keep philosophical research independent to avoid biased results that only support company goals.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Philosophy experts are playing a key role in making AI safer, smarter, and better understood by helping solve deep questions about thought and ethics in machines.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les philosophes aident les entreprises d’IA à résoudre les grandes questions sur la pensée et la sécurité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’intelligence artificielle (IA) devient de plus en plus intelligente, mais elle rencontre aussi des défis comme prendre des décisions sûres et éviter les contenus dangereux. Pour y faire face, les entreprises d’IA embauchent des philosophes, des experts qui étudient la pensée, la conscience et l’éthique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Récemment, de nombreux diplômés en philosophie ont rejoint des entreprises d’IA. Leur travail est d’aider à expliquer ce que signifie pour une machine de penser et de distinguer le bien du mal. Ils travaillent à rendre les modèles d’IA plus fiables et à empêcher qu’ils disent des choses dangereuses ou fausses. Les philosophes utilisent leurs connaissances pour guider les développeurs d’IA sur des questions complexes d’intelligence et de morale.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela apporte de nouvelles idées au développement de l’IA. Les philosophes aident les entreprises d’IA à créer de meilleures règles, difficiles à contourner, améliorant ainsi la sécurité des systèmes d’IA. Ils explorent aussi si l’IA peut être vraiment consciente, une question qui intrigue les scientifiques depuis longtemps. Cette collaboration entre la philosophie et la technologie façonne l’avenir de l’IA de manière réfléchie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les entreprises d’IA continueront de s’appuyer sur les philosophes pour améliorer la compréhension des choix éthiques par l’IA et réduire les erreurs comme les fausses informations. Les philosophes pourraient aussi aider à expliquer comment certaines parties des modèles d’IA représentent le monde, rendant l’IA plus claire et plus digne de confiance. Cependant, il faudra veiller à ce que la recherche philosophique reste indépendante pour éviter des résultats biaisés favorisant uniquement les intérêts des entreprises.

**Une phrase clé** – Les experts en philosophie jouent un rôle clé pour rendre l’IA plus sûre, plus intelligente et mieux comprise en aidant à résoudre les grandes questions sur la pensée et l’éthique des machines.
articles.personality_title_es: Los filósofos ayudan a las empresas de IA a resolver grandes preguntas sobre el pensamiento y la seguridad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inteligencia artificial (IA) se está volviendo más inteligente, pero también enfrenta desafíos como tomar decisiones seguras y evitar contenido dañino. Para resolver esto, las empresas de IA están contratando filósofos, expertos que estudian el pensamiento, la conciencia y la ética.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, muchos graduados en filosofía se han unido a empresas de IA. Su trabajo es ayudar a explicar qué significa que una máquina piense y distinga entre el bien y el mal. Trabajan para hacer que los modelos de IA sean más confiables y evitar que digan cosas peligrosas o falsas. Los filósofos usan su conocimiento para guiar a los desarrolladores de IA en preguntas complejas sobre inteligencia y moralidad.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque aporta nuevas ideas al desarrollo de la IA. Los filósofos ayudan a las empresas de IA a crear reglas mejores que no sean fáciles de engañar, mejorando la seguridad de los sistemas de IA. También exploran si la IA puede ser realmente consciente, una pregunta que ha desconcertado a los científicos por años. Esta colaboración entre filosofía y tecnología está formando el futuro de la IA de forma pensada.

**Próximo paso** – Las empresas de IA seguirán confiando en los filósofos para mejorar cómo la IA entiende las decisiones éticas y para reducir errores como la invención de información falsa. Los filósofos también podrían ayudar a explicar cómo partes de los modelos de IA representan el mundo, haciendo la IA más clara y confiable. Sin embargo, es necesario mantener la investigación filosófica independiente para evitar resultados sesgados que solo favorezcan a las empresas.

**Una frase clave** – Los expertos en filosofía están jugando un papel clave para hacer que la IA sea más segura, inteligente y mejor comprendida al ayudar a resolver preguntas profundas sobre el pensamiento y la ética en las máquinas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an abstract human brain made of interlocking puzzle pieces glowing softly, with delicate, translucent philosophical symbols—such as a balanced scale and a thoughtful eye—emanating from it, connected by fine threads to a softly glowing network of digital code patterns, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and gentle blues.
